claims.claim: A targeted abstention rule recovers near-miss accuracy to 0.998 while preserving tool-needed recall at 0.854, yielding an 81.6-point uplift over the similarity-centric baseline (stability range: 79.8–82.5 points across 8 seeded runs).

claims.claim: Standard evaluation of such agents typically reports aggregate accuracy across a mixed corpus, implicitly assuming that errors are distributed uniformly across prompt types.

evidence_refs.quote: Tool-using language model agents frequently face prompts that superficially resemble tool-call requests but in fact require direct answers. We term these *near-miss tool calls* and investigate whether they expose a tool-overcall failure mode that is invisible under standard aggregate accuracy evaluation. We construct a synthetic and weak-label dataset of 2,500 examples spanning four source types and five blind-spot categories (quoted tool requests, explicit no-tool constraints, provided observat

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The central hypothesis of this work is that near-miss examples expose a tool-overcall failure mode that is hidden by ordinary aggregate accuracy, and that a simple targeted abstention rule can substantially recover performance on this slice without catastrophically degrading tool-needed recall.
